question: Define a C++ `struct` named `Book` with members `title` (std::string), `author` (std::string), and `yearPublished` (int). Write a function `printBookDetails` that takes a `Book` struct (by value) and prints all its details. Demonstrate by creating a `Book` instance and calling the function.

test_cases.expected:
Book Details:
Title:The Code Guide
Author:A. Coder
Year Published:2024

errors: The code uses `using namespace std;` in the global scope, which is generally discouraged in larger projects as it can lead to name collisions and reduce clarity.
The `main` function and `printBookDetails` function exhibit poor and inconsistent indentation, making the code difficult to read and maintain.
The `Book` instance in `main` uses a non-descriptive variable name (`x`), which hinders code readability and understanding.

The `printBookDetails` function uses `std::endl` for every newline. While functional, `std::endl` flushes the output buffer, which can be less efficient than using `'
'` (character literal) for frequent output operations, especially in performance-critical applications.
The output formatting within `printBookDetails` lacks spaces after the colons (e.g., `Title:` instead of `Title: `), which slightly reduces readability of the printed output.
